title: Dry beans
prose: It is generally accepted that dry beans should be cultivated on fields that have previously been well-fertilized. However, given the cost price squeeze in which producers often find themselves, it frequently happens that soil fertility levels required for dry beans are not maintained. Under such circumstances, responses to direct fertilization can be expected. In addition, improved cultivation practices, including foliar feeding, have increased the potential of dry bean production. Consequently, the approach to dry bean fertilization should also be adapted. SOIL ACIDITY The optimum pH-level for dry beans is pH (KCl) 5.0, while the acid saturation percentage in the topsoil should be 0%. If the pH is lower or the acid saturation is higher than these limits, agricultural lime should be applied. Under such conditions, calcium and magnesium can often be limiting factors, and fertilization with these elements then becomes necessary. The optimum pH also results in better availability of all plant nutrients and especially micro-elements, including molybdenum, which is very important in dry bean production. (See Chapter 1.8: Soil Acidity.) Conversely, a high pH is often associated with brackish conditions. Dry beans are extremely sensitive to such conditions. (See Chapter 1.9.) PLACEMENT OF FERTILIZER Dry beans are very sensitive to fertilizer placed close to the seed. Seed burn will result in a reduced and uneven stand. If the fertilizer is not broadcast, it should be placed 5 cm away and 5 cm below the seed. Band-placement, which enhances the efficiency of nutrient uptake, is generally recommended, provided applications are not too high. With high levels of application a combination of broadcasting and band-placement is recommended. MACRO-ELEMENTS Nitrogen (N) Although good progress has been made with inoculants, successful inoculation cannot satisfy the total nitrogen requirements of dry beans. N-supplementation is therefore essential. Over-fertilization with nitrogen should, however, be avoided, since this can delay maturity and cause an increase in fungal diseases in particular. Table 5.5.2.1. Guideline for N-fertilization of dry beans Table 5.5.2.1 serves as a nitrogen guideline for dry beans. Although one ton of dry bean seeds removes between 30 and 40 kg N ha -1 , the N-recommendation will vary from field to field and from season to season. Typically, higher than median N-applications would be given on sandy soils, under conditions of lower temperatures or where poor root development may be expected (cold, wet conditions). If top-dressing is to be applied, this should be done within 3 to 4 weeks after planting to derive maximum benefit. N- application shortly before or during flowering tends to induce flower drop. Phosphorus (P) Best yields are obtained where the residual phosphorus in the soil is high as a result of good fertilizer practices on preceding crops. However, when the soil-P analysis is low to medium, an economic response to P-fertilization can be expected. It is recommended that the P-content of the soil should be at least 25 mg kg -1 (Bray 1). With soil analysis values lower than 15 mg kg -1 (Bray 1), P-fertilizers can be broadcast and supplemented with band placement. Band placement of P-fertilizer is recommended as standard practice, provided that quantities applied are not too high. Dry beans remove approximately 6 kg P per ton of beans. Table 5.5.2.2 can be used as a guideline for P-fertilization. Potassium (K) As for the other cations, the likelihood of K-deficiencies is highest on sandy soils. The soil-K analysis should not be viewed in isolation, but the ratio of potassium to the other elements should also be considered. Ideally, potassium should be not less than 8% of the cation exchange capacity (CEC). Dry beans remove approximately 18 kg K per ton of beans, and Table 5.5.2.3 can be used as a guideline for K-fertilization. Table 5.5.2.2. Guideline for P-fertilization of dry beans Table 5.5.2.3 Guideline for K-fertilization of dry beans Sulphur (S) Sulphur is sometimes referred to as the "fourth element" in plant nutrition after nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium. Sulphur is of particular importance in legumes for the protein and oil content, as well as for nitrogen fixation by Rhizobium bacteria. Sulphur is usually recommended on well-drained sandy and loamy soils. It is applied in the sulphate form (SO 4 - ). Approximately 3 kg of sulphur is removed per ton of beans. Calcium (Ca) and Magnesium (Mg) Calcium and magnesium are essential plant nutrients, and in the case of low soil analysis values, Ca and Mg can be supplemented with agricultural lime. Calcium also increases the plant's resistance to disease. As a general guideline, Ca and Mg soil test levels should be of the order of 250 and 60 mg kg -1 respectively. On heavier soils the corresponding values are 400 and 100 mg kg -1 respectively. It should also be noted that triazine residues in the soil can be activated by heavy lime applications, and could be harmful. MICRO-ELEMENTS Zinc (Zn) Zn-deficiencies usually occur in compacted soils, soils low in organic matter, sandy soils and/or soils with a high pH (KCl) > 6.0. A Zn soil analysis value of 1.5 to 2.0 mg kg -1 is regarded as adequate. Zn-containing fertilizers can be used as standard practice in dry bean production. Zinc can also be applied correctively as a foliar spray. Molybdenum (Mo) Where the soil-pH is lower than pH 5.0 (KCl), or where lime was recently applied on acid soils, seed treatment of 100 g sodium molybdate per 50 kg seed and/or a foliar spray of 100 g sodium molybdate per hectare should be applied at the four-leaf stage. Spraying three weeks later may also be necessary in some cases. Manganese (Mn) Mn-deficiencies occur on soils with a high pH and where lime was recently applied. These deficiencies can be corrected by a foliar spray. Iron (Fe) Fe-deficiencies usually occur on high pH-soils (> pH 6.4 KCl). Cold and wet conditions increase the occurrence of Fe-deficiencies. Soil application of iron is not always successful and should rather be supplemented with a foliar spray. Boron (B) Although B-deficiencies may occur, B-supplementation should be treated with great care, since dry beans are very susceptible to boron toxicity. Boron applications are not recommended for dry beans, not even if they are planted as a follow-up to sunflowers. FOLIAR SPRAYING Because of their relatively poor root system, dry beans sometimes respond well to foliar nutrition. Especially in the presence of stress factors such as cold and wet conditions, foliar nutrition may hasten the recovery of the dry bean plant. Leaf and sap analyses are useful aids in identifying nutrient deficiencies. REFERENCES ANDERSON, F.N., 1997. Fertilizing edible dry beans.
